title: “A good pun is its own reword”: Can Large Language Models Understand Puns?
research_questions: Can LLMs distinguish between puns and non-puns, and how sensitive are they to biased prompts?
Can LLMs explain the humor in puns by identifying the pun word, alternative word, pun sense, and alternative sense?
Can LLMs generate successful, funny, and original puns from a pun pair, with or without relevant contextual words?
What characteristic failure modes do LLMs exhibit across pun recognition, explanation, and generation?

datasets_used.name: Filtered SemEval-2017 Task 7 + ExPun evaluation set (Hom-Dataset and Het-Dataset)
datasets_used.size: Hom-Dataset: 10 hom-pun examples, 10 non-pun examples, 810 hom-pun test data, 633 non-pun test data; Het-Dataset: 10 het-pun examples, 10 non-pun examples, 647 het-pun test data, 499 non-pun test data

methods_proposed.name: Dual-biased prompted asking
methods_proposed.summary: For pun recognition, the paper asks each model with prompts biased toward either “pun” or “non-pun” and measures accuracy plus response consistency through ΔTPR, ΔTNR, and Cohen’s κ.

methods_proposed.name: Punchline check
methods_proposed.summary: For pun explanation, three annotators check whether explanations correctly mention the pun word, alternative word, pun sense, and alternative sense, producing Average Mention Ratio scores for each element.

methods_proposed.name: Pairwise comparison for pun explanations
methods_proposed.summary: GPT-4 compares each model explanation against the human explanation, allowing win, tie, or loss, to estimate coarse-grained explanation quality at scale.

methods_proposed.name: Free and constrained pun generation settings
methods_proposed.summary: The paper evaluates pun generation both from only the pun pair and from the pun pair plus contextual words, testing unconstrained creativity and context-conditioned generation.

methods_proposed.name: Overlap and Strict Success
methods_proposed.summary: The Overlap metric removes provided pun words, alternative words, and contextual words, then computes lexical overlap between LLM and human puns; Strict Success counts generated puns that are both successful and have overlap < 0.5.

humor_theories.detail: The paper operationalizes pun humor as double meaning through the pun pair P_p = <w_p, w_a, S_p, S_a>, where humor depends on the pun word, alternative word, pun sense, alternative sense, and contextual words.
Pun generation is evaluated using the Surprise metric from He et al. (2019), based on the unexpected presence of the pun word over an anticipated alternative word.
The generation evaluation also uses Ambiguity and Distinctiveness from Kao et al. (2016), measuring whether the sentence supports both meanings and whether those meanings are distinctive.

evaluation_methods.detail: Pun recognition uses TPR, TNR, Cohen’s κ, ΔTPR, and ΔTNR; pun generation uses Ambiguity, Distinctiveness, Surprise, One-pun-word Incorporation Rate, Contextual Word Incorporation Rate, Overlap, and Strict Success.
Three English-major undergraduate annotators evaluated 100 hom-puns and 100 het-puns for punchline checks; their annotations had Fleiss’s κ = 0.87, and disagreements were resolved by majority view.
The same subset of 100 hom-puns and 100 het-puns was used for manual pun generation evaluation, where annotators judged Success Rate and rated Funniness from 1 to 5.
GPT-4 performed pairwise comparisons between human and model explanations, choosing a winner or tie; GPT-4 achieved 88.3% consistency with annotators on sampled data.

headline_results.finding: With the enhanced prompt, GPT-4-Turbo achieved the strongest pun-recognition consistency on homographic puns.
With the enhanced prompt, GPT-4-Turbo also achieved the strongest recognition consistency on heterographic puns.
In punchline checking, GPT-4-Turbo best captured homographic pun elements among LLMs, while human explanations still led on the alternative sense.
headline_results.metric: Average Mention Ratio for w_p / w_a / S_p / S_a

headline_results.finding: For heterographic pun explanations, GPT-4-Turbo and Claude-3-Opus were strongest among LLMs but still trailed humans on the hidden alternative word and sense.
headline_results.metric: Average Mention Ratio for w_p / w_a / S_p / S_a

headline_results.finding: In constrained homographic pun generation, GPT-4-Turbo had the best Success Rate and Funniness among LLMs, but remained below human puns.
In constrained heterographic pun generation, Claude-3-Opus had the best Success Rate, while Claude-3-Opus and GPT-4-Turbo tied on Funniness, still below humans.

key_findings: Most tested LLMs were influenced by prompt bias in pun recognition, and adding a pun definition plus examples improved consistency and non-pun recognition.
TNR was generally lower than TPR because the non-puns were mostly non-pun jokes and proverbs, making them similar to puns.
The paper found no obvious difference between hom-puns and het-puns in recognition, but het-pun explanation and generation were harder.
Most LLMs, except GPT-4-Turbo and Claude-3-Opus, struggled to identify alternative words and alternative senses in het-puns because the alternative word is not directly present in the text.
Providing relevant contextual words improved generation quality and success rate, but increased the chance that LLMs reproduced human puns.
LLMs often used a “lazy pun generation” pattern, placing multiple pun or alternative words in different parts of the sentence rather than combining both meanings into one punchline.
limitations: All evaluated pun texts are in English, and the authors note that pun understanding may vary across languages with different definitions, structures, or purposes.
The Overlap metric is only a rough plagiarism/originality indicator because LLM training data are largely unavailable and models might copy puns not present in the authors’ dataset.
Evaluation of pun explanation quality and generated pun success depends on human judgments, and annotator preferences for detail, clarity, brevity, and humor may vary.
The authors note that a pun amusing to one person may offend another.
The authors cannot guarantee that the publicly sourced pun datasets are free from socially harmful or toxic language.
safety_ethics_notes: The paper states that annotators were recruited by the authors’ institution, paid $9/h exceeding the local minimum wage, consented to the use of their generated pun data, and had their privacy respected. The authors acknowledge possible socially harmful or toxic language in public pun datasets and note that humor judgments vary across backgrounds. They also state that ChatGPT was used to correct grammatical errors in the paper.

artifacts.other: Prompts are provided in Appendix B; annotation interface screenshots are shown in Figures 6, 7, and 8.

human_samples.population: undergraduates who majored in English; described as very familiar with puns and specifically trained for the evaluation work
human_samples.observations: Punchline-check annotations on 100 randomly selected hom-puns and 100 het-puns; manual pun-generation evaluation on the same subset, judging success and funniness on a 1–5 scale; sampled pairwise-comparison judgments used to validate GPT-4 judging, but the exact validation sample size is not stated.
human_samples.detail: Appendix A reports three English-major undergraduate annotators, paid $9/h, above the local minimum wage. The paper reports Fleiss’s κ = 0.87 for punchline-check annotations and majority vote for disagreements. The ethics statement says privacy was respected and annotators consented to use of the pun data generated by them for research purposes.

human_samples.population: crowdsourced annotators in the ExPUNations dataset used by the paper
human_samples.observations: Provided detailed annotations, including human explanations and keyword sets, for pun entries used in the filtered evaluation data; the authors selected the longest explanation and most extensive keyword set where applicable.
human_samples.detail: The paper states that ExPun augments SemEval-2017 Task 7 with detailed crowdsourced annotations, but does not report the original annotator counts, demographics, recruitment, or geography.

human_samples.population: creators or collectors of public English puns, non-pun jokes, and proverbs in SemEval-2017 Task 7 and related source material
human_samples.observations: Filtered data include Hom-Dataset examples: 10 hom-pun examples, 10 non-pun examples, 810 hom-pun test items, 633 non-pun test items; Het-Dataset examples: 10 het-pun examples, 10 non-pun examples, 647 het-pun test items, 499 non-pun test items. Human pun texts from the dataset are also used as generation references/baselines.
human_samples.detail: The paper describes the pun datasets as publicly available and sourced from prior datasets; prior work is described as collecting puns from literature and the Internet. Original creator counts and demographics are not reported.

judges.name: SemEval-2017 Task 7 / ExPUNations source annotations
judges.detail: Gold/source labels and annotations from the public datasets provide pun/non-pun status, pun pairs, human explanations, and keyword/contextual-word sets used to construct the benchmark and evaluate model outputs. The original annotation procedures are not detailed in this paper.

judges.name: three English-major undergraduate annotators
judges.detail: For punchline checks, annotators judged whether each explanation correctly mentioned the pun word, alternative word, pun sense, and alternative sense. Disagreements were resolved by majority view; Fleiss’s κ = 0.87 is reported.

judges.name: three English-major undergraduate annotators
judges.detail: For pun-generation evaluation on the same 100 hom-pun and 100 het-pun subset, annotators identified whether a generated text was a successful pun and rated funniness from 1 to 5.

judges.name: three English-major undergraduate annotators
judges.detail: Human pairwise-comparison judgments on sampled data were used to validate GPT-4 as a pairwise explanation judge; the paper reports GPT-4 accuracy/consistency of 88.3%, but not the exact validation sample size or protocol.

judges.name: GPT-4
judges.detail: Used for large-scale coarse-grained pairwise comparison between a human explanation and a model explanation, allowing win, tie/unsure, or loss. The final pairwise-comparison prompt was 0-shot after preliminary 0-shot and 3-shot experiments showed similar performance.

judges.name: GPT-4
judges.detail: Used to find synonyms for pun words and alternative words in homographic puns so that Ambiguity, Distinctiveness, Surprise, and related automatic metrics could be computed when pun word and alternative word are identical.

judges.name: recognition metrics
judges.detail: Pun recognition is scored with TPR, TNR, Cohen’s κ, ΔTPR, and ΔTNR against the dataset labels under dual-biased prompts.

judges.name: generation and originality metrics
judges.detail: Pun generation is evaluated with Ambiguity, Distinctiveness, Surprise, One-pun-word Incorporation Rate, Contextual Word Incorporation Rate, Overlap, and Strict Success. Strict Success combines human success judgments with Overlap < 0.5.

judges.name: paper authors
judges.detail: Authors reviewed and filtered the public datasets to ensure required fields were present, selected demonstrations for prompts, categorized qualitative explanation errors, and identified the 'lazy pun generation' pattern.

generation_selection.scope: pun explanation outputs collected as the Reason field from enhanced CoT recognition/explanation prompts
generation_selection.n: one explanation/reason requested per call; exact final bias setting not reported
generation_selection.detail: No best-of-N generation or reranking is reported for explanations. Recognition/explanation temperature is stated as 0.

generation_selection.scope: pun generation with only the pun pair
generation_selection.n: one JSON sentence requested per item/model
generation_selection.detail: The prompt asks for a single pun sentence using the pun pair, with 3 examples in the prompt and temperature 0.7. No candidate pool, retries, or selection procedure is reported.

generation_selection.scope: pun generation with pun pair plus relevant contextual words
generation_selection.n: one JSON sentence requested per item/model
generation_selection.detail: The constrained prompt asks for a single pun sentence using the contextual words, with 3 examples in the prompt and temperature 0.7. No best-of-N selection is reported.

generation_selection.n: one JSON sentence requested per item
generation_selection.detail: GPT-3.5-Turbo is asked to generate non-puns containing the same pun words as human puns as a lower-bound reference; no examples are provided and no selection procedure is reported.

generation_selection.scope: GPT-4 synonym generation for homographic-pun automatic metrics
generation_selection.n: two synonyms requested per item, one for each meaning
generation_selection.detail: The prompt asks GPT-4 to output synonyms for both meanings of a homographic pun word; no candidate selection or human validation of synonym choices is reported.

model_provenance.name: Llama2-7B-Chat
model_provenance.detail: Open-source 7B model evaluated; checkpoint, serving method, and inference date are not reported.

model_provenance.name: Mistral-7B
model_provenance.detail: Open-source 7B model evaluated; exact checkpoint/instruction variant, serving method, and inference date are not reported.

model_provenance.name: Vicuna-7B
model_provenance.detail: Open-source 7B model evaluated; exact checkpoint, serving method, and inference date are not reported.

model_provenance.name: OpenChat-7B
model_provenance.detail: Open-source 7B model evaluated; exact checkpoint, serving method, and inference date are not reported.

model_provenance.name: Gemini-Pro
model_provenance.detail: Closed-source model evaluated; no API snapshot, access date, or inference date is reported.

model_provenance.name: GPT-3.5-Turbo
model_provenance.detail: Closed-source model evaluated and also used to generate non-pun baseline sentences; no API snapshot, access date, or inference date is reported.

model_provenance.name: Claude-3-Opus
model_provenance.detail: Closed-source model evaluated; no API snapshot, access date, or inference date is reported.

model_provenance.name: GPT-4-Turbo
model_provenance.detail: Closed-source model evaluated; no API snapshot, access date, or inference date is reported.

model_provenance.name: GPT-4
model_provenance.detail: Used as an LLM judge for pairwise explanation comparison and as a synonym generator for homographic-pun metric computation; no exact version, snapshot, access date, or inference date is reported.

model_provenance.name: SkipGram model
model_provenance.detail: Used to compute Ambiguity and Distinctiveness; the paper cites Mikolov et al. (2013) but does not report a checkpoint, training corpus, or implementation details beyond limited vocabulary handling.

model_provenance.name: LM pre-trained on WikiText
model_provenance.detail: Used to compute Surprise; the paper cites WikiText work and reports excluding the top 2% of extreme Surprise values, but not the exact LM checkpoint or implementation.

inference_budgets.scope: pun recognition with basic and enhanced dual-biased prompts
inference_budgets.calls_per_item: 2 calls per prompt type are implied by the pun-biased and non-pun-biased instructions; both basic and enhanced prompt types are evaluated
inference_budgets.detail: The enhanced prompt adds a pun definition and 6 examples; the basic prompt includes instruction and test data. ΔTPR and ΔTNR compare the non-pun-biased prompt to the pun-biased prompt. Max tokens, retries, invalid-output handling, and cost are not reported.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: not fully reported; direct-answer and CoT response methods are compared under the enhanced prompt with bias toward pun for four selected models
inference_budgets.detail: The paper reports this as an ablation for Llama2-7B-Chat, Mistral-7B, Gemini-Pro, and GPT-4-Turbo; exact dataset coverage and total calls are not stated.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: not fully reported; explanations are collected from enhanced prompts for best performance
inference_budgets.detail: The paper says explanations are the Reason part of the CoT recognition prompt. It does not specify which bias setting was used for the final explanation set or how malformed outputs were handled.

inference_budgets.scope: pun generation with only pun pair
inference_budgets.calls_per_item: not explicitly stated; prompt requests one sentence per item/model
inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: temperature = 0.7; 3 in-context examples; parsable JSON output requested
inference_budgets.detail: No best-of-N, reranking, max token budget, retry policy, or cost is reported.

inference_budgets.scope: pun generation with pun pair and relevant contextual words
inference_budgets.calls_per_item: not explicitly stated; prompt requests one sentence per item/model
inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: temperature = 0.7; 3 in-context examples; contextual words included; parsable JSON output requested
inference_budgets.detail: The prompt instructs models to use all contextual words and avoid reusing the keyword later in the sentence. No best-of-N, reranking, max token budget, retry policy, or cost is reported.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: not explicitly stated; prompt requests one sentence per item
inference_budgets.detail: The non-pun prompt has no examples. Temperature and other decoding settings for this baseline are not separately reported.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: 1 judgment call per explanation pair is implied; exact total comparisons not reported here
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: not applicable as generation candidates; judge chooses one of three labels
inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: 0-shot was chosen to save token usage; actual token use and cost are not reported
inference_budgets.detail: Preliminary 0-shot and 3-shot settings had similar performance; final evaluation used 0-shot. Exact GPT-4 snapshot and decoding settings are not reported.

inference_budgets.scope: GPT-4 synonym generation for homographic-pun metrics
inference_budgets.calls_per_item: not reported; prompt requests synonyms for each homographic-pun item
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: one synonym for each of two meanings requested
inference_budgets.detail: Prompt includes 6 examples. Exact model version, temperature, validation, and handling of bad synonym outputs are not reported.

inference_budgets.scope: automatic Ambiguity, Distinctiveness, and Surprise metric computation
inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: not applicable for reported SkipGram/WikiText metric models; top 2% of extreme Surprise values excluded
inference_budgets.detail: Metrics are computed only for generations whose words are within the models’ vocabularies, but the number of excluded generations and exact model checkpoints are not reported.

human_baselines.description: No independent human pun-recognition baseline is reported; LLMs are scored against dataset labels.
human_baselines.detail: The study reports TPR, TNR, κ, and prompt-bias sensitivity for LLMs, not human recognizers.

human_baselines.description: Human explanations from ExPUNations are used as a baseline/reference in punchline checks and as the comparison target in GPT-4 pairwise evaluation.
human_baselines.detail: Human and model explanations are evaluated on the same puns, but the human explanations are pre-existing crowdsourced dataset annotations; the paper does not report that humans and models produced explanations under materially matched prompts or conditions. The authors selected the longest available explanation.

human_baselines.description: Human pun texts from the filtered dataset are evaluated as 'Human pun' in Table 4 and compared with LLM-generated puns on Success and Funniness.
human_baselines.detail: The human puns are source/reference puns rather than outputs generated under the same prompt. In the constrained setting, LLMs are given contextual words derived from the human puns, making conditions unmatched.

human_baselines.description: The lower-bound non-pun baseline is generated by GPT-3.5-Turbo, not by humans.
human_baselines.detail: The paper asks GPT-3.5-Turbo to generate non-puns containing the same pun words as human puns.

mitigations: The paper introduces an Overlap metric comparing LLM-generated puns with human puns after removing prompt-provided pun words, alternative words, and contextual words.
Strict Success requires both manual success and Overlap < 0.5.
The authors explicitly state that Overlap is only a rough originality/plagiarism indicator and cannot rule out copying puns not present in the dataset.
contamination_risk.basis: The evaluation is built from public legacy datasets: SemEval-2017 Task 7 and ExPUNations, with puns ultimately sourced from public materials such as literature and the Internet. These resources predate several evaluated 2023–2024 proprietary models, and the paper reports no decontamination or pretraining-overlap test for recognition, explanation, or generation. Human explanations and keyword sets from ExPun may also have been public before some model training. Generation prompts sometimes include contextual words derived from the reference human pun, and the authors observe increased reproduction risk in that setting. The Overlap/Strict Success analysis is a useful but explicitly limited mitigation, so contamination remains a high risk for memorization-inflated performance or copying.

llm_judge_dependence.affected_claims: Large-scale pairwise explanation-quality win/tie/loss results, including claims that GPT-4-Turbo and Claude-3-Opus explanations often approach or surpass human explanations; homographic-pun automatic generation metrics that depend on GPT-4-generated synonyms.
llm_judge_dependence.human_validation: GPT-4 pairwise comparison is reported to achieve 88.3% accuracy/consistency with the authors’ annotators on sampled data. The fine-grained punchline check and generation success/funniness are human-annotated. No human validation is reported for GPT-4 synonym generation.
llm_judge_dependence.detail: The paper’s overall conclusions also rely on non-LLM automatic recognition metrics and human evaluations, so LLM judging is not the sole basis of the study. However, GPT-4 judging determines the large-scale pairwise explanation analysis, and GPT-4-generated synonyms feed some automatic metrics, making LLM-judge dependence substantial rather than merely incidental.

reporting_gaps: Exact model snapshots, checkpoint identifiers, API versions, access dates, and inference dates are not reported for the evaluated LLMs or GPT-4 judge/synonym generator.
Access method and serving setup are not reported for either open-source or closed-source models; hardware, software environment, compute, and cost are also not reported.
Token budgets, max output lengths, stop criteria, retry policies, and malformed-JSON handling are not reported.

Decoding parameters are only partly reported: temperature = 0 for recognition/explanation and temperature = 0.7 for pun generation. Other sampling parameters are missing, and temperature for GPT-4 judging, GPT-4 synonym generation, and GPT-3.5-Turbo non-pun generation is not separately stated.

The exact number of calls per item is not fully specified for explanation collection, generation, GPT-4 pairwise comparisons, and synonym generation; prompts request one output, but retry/candidate handling is not described.
The final bias setting used to collect explanation Reason fields from the enhanced recognition/explanation prompt is not specified.
The exact sample size and protocol for validating GPT-4 pairwise comparison against human annotators are not reported.
Blinding, randomization, model-identity masking, and explanation-order randomization for human annotation and pairwise validation are not reported.
Inter-annotator agreement is reported for punchline checks only; agreement for generation success, funniness ratings, and pairwise-validation annotations is not reported.
Original SemEval/ExPun annotator or contributor counts, demographics, recruitment, and geography are not reported in this paper.
No decontamination, memorization, or pretraining-overlap test is reported for the public benchmark items or ExPun explanations; the Overlap metric is acknowledged as rough.
GPT-4 synonym outputs used for homographic-pun metrics are not validated, and the exact GPT-4 version and inference settings are missing.
SkipGram and WikiText LM metric implementations are underspecified: exact checkpoints, training data/version, vocabularies, and number of excluded out-of-vocabulary generations are not reported.
Human explanation and human pun baselines are pre-existing dataset artifacts; the paper does not document materially matched human prompt conditions for direct comparison with LLM outputs.
